does: This paper discovers and characterizes polymorphism—the phenomenon where independently trained transformers implement identical functions but in rotated basis spaces. The authors demonstrate this across models from two-layer networks to Pythia-70m by showing that a single orthogonal Procrustes fit computed from one batch of activations can align the hidden representations between independently trained models. They show that sparse-autoencoder (SAE) feature dictionaries and steering vectors transfer between models with this alignment and no retraining. Critically, they reveal that standard SAE universality metrics fail to detect this problem: decoder columns align at 98% cosine similarity while encoder reconstructions achieve negative explained variance, worse than baseline. Applying a single rotation R restores reconstruction quality to near within-seed performance across all layers, with R following a Haar distribution matching random orthogonal predictions.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability program, particularly building on recent research into SAE universality and the transferability of learned features across models. It responds directly to claims that SAEs learn universal features, showing these claims rest on a measurement blind spot. The paper connects to prior work on rotational invariance in neural networks and the geometry of learned representations, while engaging with steering and intervention-based interpretability methods that assume feature alignment. It builds on foundational work showing that different random initializations lead to different internal coordinate systems, pushing beyond superficial universality toward deeper structural understanding of model invariances.
enables: This finding opens directions for more robust mechanistic interpretability work: SAE metrics must account for rotational degrees of freedom when claiming universality, requiring either rotation-invariant similarity measures or explicit alignment. It enables practical improvements to interpretability tools by showing that Procrustes alignment is a simple preprocessing step that fixes feature transfer and steering across models. The work suggests deeper investigation into the geometric structure of learned representations and whether other transformations beyond rotation explain cross-model differences. It points toward developing more sensitive diagnostic tools for mechanistic interpretability and toward understanding whether the rotational invariance observed here extends to larger models, different architectures, or other domains, potentially reshaping how we validate interpretability claims.
